heading: DI TUTTE LE OPERE DANDO A TUTTI
card_summary: This chunk opens the sixth "tone" (Tono Sesto) of the Secondo Cantico of De Harmonia Mundi, under the heading "La facolta di operare" (the faculty of operating/working). Zorzi's central argument, drawn from Paul's Letter to the Hebrews and developed through Pseudo-Dionysius's Divine Names (citing Hierotheus), Origen, Plato's Parmenides and Epistles, and Kabbalistic sefirot, is that Christ is the author and perfecter of all faith and all creaturely operations. He then elaborates Christ's omnipresence as the metaphysical plenitudo of all things by identifying him with every element, metal, plant, animal, celestial body, and planet, as well as with artificial and divine constructs (tabernacle, house of God, the Hebrew letter Beth). The Trinitarian structure maps onto the Neoplatonic triad of One/Being/Good: the Father fills with unity, the Son with being and truth, the Spirit with goodness. The Kabbalistic "ten elements" of Hebrew theology are read as pointing to Christ as the Verbum incarnatum, invoking Baruch 3 and Dionysian thearchia.
essay:
# DI TUTTE LE OPERE DANDO A TUTTI — Chunk 79 (pp. 1739–1808)

**Note:** This summary covers the opening portion of a very long section (27,630 words). The analysis is based on the first approximately 500 lines of the text, which correspond to the introductory chapters of the Tono Sesto and its subsections up through Chapter V. The remaining pages, which continue the enumeration of Christ's names and the transition to the theme of "giving to all," are not covered here.

---

## Introduction: The Sixth Tone and the Faculty of Operating

The Tono Sesto (sixth tone) of the Secondo Cantico represents a turning point in Zorzi's systematic theology. Having established in preceding books the cosmological architecture of the universe—its grounding in divine ideas, the hierarchical emanation of forms, and the role of celestial influences—Zorzi now pivots to the practical and operative dimension: *la facoltà di operare*, the faculty of all action and operation. This shift from speculative to operative metaphysics is not merely a change of subject matter but reflects a fundamental commitment to Zorzi's entire project: to demonstrate that harmony is not only the formal structure of creation but also the active principle governing all creaturely agency. The opening chapter establishes that Christ, the incarnate Word, is not merely the object of belief among many objects but the very source and completion (*author* and *consummator*) of both faith itself and all the deeds that faith generates.

This ontological claim rests on a sophisticated synthesis of Pauline theology, Neoplatonic metaphysics, and Hebrew mystical philosophy. Zorzi's method is characteristically systematic: he works from Scripture as the governing authority, then enlists philosophical tradition (particularly the Platonic and Neoplatonic schools) as supporting witnesses, and finally demonstrates how the esoteric doctrines of the Kabbalah—the sefirot and the Hebrew letter-theology—corroborate the same truth. The result is a comprehensive Christology in which Christ functions simultaneously as the metaphysical principle of being (*esse*), the formal cause of all determinate things, and the living completion of all human and divine action.

---

## Christ as Author and Perfecter of Faith: The Pauline Foundation

Zorzi grounds his argument in Hebrews 12:2, one of the most christologically dense passages in the New Testament. Paul exhorts the faithful: *"Let us run with perseverance the race set before us, keeping our eyes fixed on Jesus, author and perfecter of faith."* <cite>p. 1571</cite> This verse is not cited casually; it serves as the governing *lemma* (foundational theorem) from which all subsequent claims proceed. Zorzi's hermeneutical strategy here is to distinguish between two orders of knowledge: natural knowledge and revealed knowledge.

Natural reason (*discorso naturale*), operating through the faculties of perception and rational inference, can demonstrate that God exists and created the world. Even Aristotle, "if awakened from the sleep of the senses" (*se risvegliato dal sonno delle percezioni*), would concede this point. Aristotle, though limited to sensible perception in his natural philosophy, shares with other wise men the recognition of God as the Artificer (*Artefice*) of the universe. This is significant: Zorzi grants that pagan philosophy can arrive at a monotheistic theology through natural reason alone. However—and here lies the distinctive content of Christian theology—that *God created all things through the incarnate Word*, that is, through Jesus Christ (*Dio abbia creato il tutto per mezzo del Verbo incarnato, che è GESÙ Cristo*), is knowable only through Sacred Scripture and divine revelation.

This epistemological boundary is crucial to understanding Zorzi's entire theological edifice. He is not denying the power of reason; rather, he is locating the Incarnation properly within revelation. Natural reason can reach God as creator; only Scripture reveals God as creating through the Word made flesh. Zorzi then performs a comprehensive proof through the Apostles' Creed, demonstrating that all twelve articles either refer to Christ, emanate from him, or lead back to him (*Omnia credenda aut sunt circa Christum, aut emanant ab ipso, aut ad eundem referuntur*). <cite>p. 1573</cite> This rhetorical device shows that Christ is not one article of faith alongside others but the unifying center and measure of the entire faith. The Creed becomes, in Zorzi's hands, an instrument for revealing the structural centrality of Christ to Christian belief.

Moreover, Zorzi extends this principle to action and commandment: "all things that must be done and all commandments, though they may appear foreign and separate from him, nevertheless cannot be carried out without him, who says: 'Without me you can do nothing.'" <cite>p. 1573</cite> This application of John 15:5 is essential. Christ is not merely the formal object of belief but the active power within all creaturely operations. The faculty of faith is not simply an assent to propositions about Christ; it is participation in Christ's own operative power.

---

## The Kabbalah and the Transmission of Divine Wisdom

A pivotal hermeneutical move occurs when Zorzi introduces the theology of the Hebrews (*la teologia degli ebrei*) and its "ten elements" (*dieci elementi*)—the sefirot of the Kabbalah. He cites Baruch 3:37 (*"Hic adinvenit omnem viam disciplinae, et tradidit eam Iahacob puero suo"*—"He found the way of knowledge and gave it to Jacob his servant"), interpreted as proof that the divine Wisdom transmitted in Hebrew tradition was the same Logos that became incarnate as Jesus Christ. <cite>p. 1573</cite> This move is characteristic of Zorzi's ecumenical approach: the sefirot of the mystical tradition, rather than representing a competing or merely parallel wisdom, are shown to point forward to their ultimate fulfillment in Christ.

The ten sefirot—*Keter* (Crown), *Chokmah* (Wisdom), *Binah* (Understanding), *Chesed* (Mercy), *Gevurah* (Severity), *Tiphareth* (Beauty), *Netzach* (Victory), *Hod* (Splendor), *Yesod* (Foundation), and *Malkuth* (Kingdom)—constitute the architectural blueprint of the Kabbalah's cosmos. Each sefirah represents both a divine emanation and a mode of being within creation. By arguing that these ten elements find their source and completion in Christ, Zorzi assimilates Kabbalistic cosmology into a Christological framework. The sefirot cease to be understood as independent principles and become, rather, crystallizations of the single divine principle: the Incarnate Word. This is not syncretism in the pejorative sense but a deliberate theological strategy to show that even the esoteric doctrines of Jewish mysticism converge on the mystery of Christ.

---

## The Trinitarian Distribution of Metaphysical Gifts

Chapter II shifts to a systematic exposition of the Trinity as the principle of all metaphysical donation. Here Zorzi develops an ontological schema indebted to both Origen and the entire Neoplatonic tradition, particularly as mediated through Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite.

Following Origen (as Zorzi explicitly notes), the three divine Persons distribute three fundamental metaphysical gifts:

- From the **Father**, we receive *being as unity* (*esse*). The Father is the source (*prima omnium origo*) from which all reality flows.
- From the **Son**, we receive *intelligibility* (*intelligere*) and ordered existence (*ordinata*). The Son, as supreme Wisdom (*sapienza suprema*), is the principle of formal determination and rational order.
- From the **Holy Spirit**, we receive sanctification (*sanctificazione*) and goodness (*bontà*). The Spirit is the vivifying principle that actualizes potential and perfects all things through donation of themselves to themselves.

Zorzi then invokes the ancient theologians (particularly the Neoplatonic school) who taught that *the One* precedes being. This doctrine—fundamental to Plotinus and his successors—holds that the ultimate principle transcends even the category of being (*on*). Being, in this schema, is the first emanation or hypostasis from the One. Zorzi maps this Neoplatonic triad onto the Christian Trinity:

> «Come l'Uno, l'Ente e il Bene sono la stessa cosa, come affermano quanti insegnano i principi della dialettica, così il Padre, il Figlio e lo Spirito santo sono una cosa sola quanto all'essenza, benché siano distinti nelle loro relazioni.»

<cite>p. 1577</cite>

This is a bold identification. The Neoplatonic transcendentals—the One, Being (*on*), and the Good (*agathon*)—are equated with the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. The Father, as *prima origo* (first origin), corresponds to the One; the Son, as the supreme Wisdom in whom all being is contained, corresponds to Being; and the Spirit, who distributes goodness to all creatures, corresponds to the Good. Zorzi is not claiming identity between Neoplatonism and Christianity but rather demonstrating that pagan philosophy, in its highest reaches, intuited the structure that Christianity fulfills and reveals.

The distribution of these gifts is explained through the metaphor of emanation: Unity emanating from the Father fills all things so that each thing is *one* (has unity and numerical identity). Being, emanating from the Son and communicated through him to all creatures, enables them to *be* and *exist*. Goodness, from the Spirit, fills all things with perfection and actualization. This is the metaphysical foundation for the operative faculty: all action is possible only through participation in these three divine donations.

---

## Christ as Plenitudo: The Dionysian Framework

The centerpiece of this section is an extended quotation from Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite's *De divinis nominibus* (On the Divine Names), specifically Hierotheus's characterization of Jesus's deity. Hierotheus, presented by Dionysius as the supreme theologian and exemplar of theurgic knowledge, describes Christ in language of incomparable density:

> «La divinità di Gesù è causa di tutte le cose e riempie tutto»

Christ's divinity is simultaneously *causa* (cause) and *plenitudo* (fullness/plenitude). More specifically, Hierotheus enumerates a cascade of christological titles and offices:

- **For the fallen**: Christ is "the recall and the resurrection of those who have fallen low" (*revocatio, atque resurrectio*), restoring what was lost and raising what was fallen.
- **For those corrupted**: Christ is "the renewal and reformation of those who have lost the divine image in themselves" (*renovatio, atque reformatio*), re-establishing the *imago Dei*.
- **For the agitated**: Christ is "the sacred stabilization of those shaken by profane agitation" (*confirmatio sacra*), providing ontological security.
- **For the standing**: Christ is "the steady safety of those who endure" (*tuta securitas*), preserving beings in their being.
- **For those ascending**: Christ is "the extended hand reaching out to those who hasten toward him" (*porrecta manu productio*), offering grace and aid.
- **For the illuminated**: Christ is "the radiance of those illuminated" (*fulgor illuminatorum*).
- **For the perfected**: Christ is "the principle of perfection itself of those being perfected" (*principatus ipse perfectionis*).
- **For the deified**: Christ is "the thearchia of those being deified" (*thearchia eorum qui deificantur*). This term, *thearchia*, is crucial: it denotes not merely the rule (*archia*) of God (*theos*) but the divinization or deification (*theosis*) of creatures through union with the divine.

Zorzi then synthesizes these titles into a supreme formula: Christ is "the life of the living, the substance of the existing, the principle and cause of every life and substance." (*viventium vita, existentium substantia, omnis vitae, et substantiae principium, et causa*) <cite>p. 1577</cite>

This Dionysian language is not merely rhetorical flourish. It expresses a profound metaphysical claim: Christ is the *on* (being itself), the very principle of existence. He contains all things eminently—not as parts contained in a whole, but as the form-giving principle that makes all differentiated things possible. Dionysius's hierarchy of names—from the fallen to the perfected—maps onto the Neoplatonic hierarchy of beings, with Christ standing at the apex as the source and goal of all movement.

---

## The Paradox of Transcendence and Immanence: Neither Part nor Whole

Chapter III wrestles with an apparent paradox: If Christ is "all in all," how is he neither part nor whole? Dionysius, as transmitted through Hierotheus, offers a resolution: "Jesus is neither part nor whole, and yet is wholly both part and whole, grasping and pre-containing all parts and the whole in himself." (*Jesus neque pars est, neque totum, et tamen est totum, et pars, omnium partem, et totum in seipso accipiens, et praehabens.*)  <cite>p. 1579</cite>

Zorzi unpacks this paradox through a series of analogies:

1. **The Perfect Gem in a Rotten Casket**: A flawless gem placed in a decaying wooden box loses nothing of its perfection, yet does not shine as brilliantly as it would in gold. Analogously, Christ's divinity, though present in imperfect creatures, operates according to their capacity, not with the supernal radiance it possesses in itself.

2. **The Sun and All Living Things**: The sun produces all plants and animals without itself being a plant or animal. It gives the heat and light necessary for their life without partaking of their particularity. Christ, as the intelligible sun, is the formal principle of all creation without being limited by any created form.

3. **Sunlight Through Mud**: As sunlight passes through mud without being polluted by it, Christ's operative presence illuminates all things without contamination or mixture. This image emphasizes the doctrine of *simplicity*: Christ's nature, being utterly simple and infinite, cannot be diminished or altered by contact with the complex and finite.

Zorzi then applies political analogies:

> «È la forma che crea e induce la forma nelle cose informi, in quanto vero Datore delle forme e il Principe della forma che non coincide con alcuna forma determinata, ma è al di sopra di tutte le forme, come il sole riscalda con il suo favore tutte le erbe e gli animali che devono vivere senza essere un animale o una pianta.»

<cite>p. 1579</cite>

An emperor orders all his commanders while transcending every command; a pope orders all bishops while being a bishop yet transcending every episcopate. The hierarchical structure is maintained—obedience to Christ as the supreme principle does not eliminate the particular offices and dignities of creation—yet Christ remains utterly transcendent. This political theology, standard in medieval Christendom, serves Zorzi's purpose of showing that metaphysical transcendence and providential order are not opposites but aspects of a single divine governance.

---

## The Encyclopaedia of Christ's Names: Scripture and Cosmos

Chapters IV and V constitute an extensive scriptural and cosmological dossier, organized hierarchically from the lowest to the highest creatures. This encyclopaedic project—which Zorzi acknowledges will be extensive—aims to demonstrate that every created thing, named according to its essential nature, is a manifestation of Christ.

### Elementary Substances

Zorzi begins with the elements (*elementa*), the fundamental principles of material composition:

- **Earth** (*terra*): The true earth that "flows with milk of superior wisdom and honey of divinity" is Christ, as the Psalmist testifies: *"The Lord will give goodness, and our earth shall give its fruit."* (Ps. 85:12)
- **Water**: Elaborated earlier in the work, water signifies Christ's cleansing and vivifying power.
- **Air** (*aer*) and **Breath** (*flatus*): When Christ breathed upon the disciples saying *"Receive the Holy Spirit"* (John 20:22), he gave them the very principle of divine life. The Latin *spiritus* (spirit) and *flatus* (breath) are etymologically identical, expressing the notion that the Holy Spirit is the divine respiration.
- **Fire** (*ignis*): Christ proclaimed, *"I have come to cast fire on the earth, that it may burn"* (Luke 12:49). This fire represents both judgment and purification, the divine ardor that consumes all that is base.

### Plants and Organic Life

- **Grain of Wheat**: Christ himself stated, *"Unless a grain of wheat falls into the ground and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it brings forth much fruit."* (John 12:24) This image conflates the sacramental bread of the Eucharist with Christ's sacrificial death and resurrection.
- **Tree of Knowledge and Tree of Life**: Christ is identified with both trees of paradise. The Tree of Knowledge represents Christ as the source of all understanding; the Tree of Life offers the wood necessary for the "building of the true edifice" (*veri aedificii*) that each soul must construct.
- **Vine** (*vitis*): In the *Johannine* tradition (John 15), Christ explicitly identifies himself as the true vine, and the believer as the branch; the Father is the vinedresser.

### Minerals and Precious Substances

- **Stone and Rock** (*lapis, petra*): Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 10:4, *"That rock was Christ,"* upon which the Church is founded (Eph. 2:20). Christ is the *cornerstone* (*angularis lapis*) that joins all things together, the foundation upon which all things stand.
- **Gold** (*aurum*): Christ says, *"I advise you to buy from me gold refined by fire"* (Rev. 3:18), the incorruptible treasure that survives all temporal loss.
- **Silver** (*argentum*): The Psalmist declares, *"The words of the Lord are pure, silver tried in the fire, purified seven times"* (Ps. 12:6). What is the true Word of the Lord if not Christ, "the Word made flesh" (*verbum caro factum*)? (John 1:1, 14)

### Animals: The Hierarchy of Creaturely Kinds

Zorzi moves through the animal kingdom with systematic precision:

- **Lion** (*leo*): "The Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David has conquered" (Rev. 5:5). Christ is the apex of all fierceness and strength, the judge and warrior.
- **Ox/Calf** (*bos, vitulus*): In Luke's Gospel, the father kills "the fattened calf" (*vitulum saginatum*) in celebration of the prodigal son's return. This calf is traditionally interpreted as a figure of Christ, the sacrifice offered in joy at the restoration of sinners.
- **Lamb** (*agnus*): The most exalted animal title. John the Baptist, seeing Jesus, proclaims: *"Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!"* (John 1:29) The Lamb evokes the Passover sacrifice and, in the Apocalypse, the Lamb is the ultimate liturgical center of heaven itself (Rev. 5, 7).
- **Eagle** (*aquila*): Queen of birds. Solomon counts among the four most difficult things to understand "the way of an eagle in the sky" (Prov. 30:19). The eagle, as protector of its young, spreading its wings to shield them from arrows while absorbing the wounds itself, is a figure of Christ's protective love. Isaiah writes of Christ, *"He was wounded for our transgressions"* (Isa. 53:5); Moses in his canticle sings, *"Like an eagle that stirs up its nest, that flutters over its young, spreading out its wings, catching them, bearing them on its pinions"* (Deut. 32:11). This is Christ bearing humanity on the wings of divine love.

### Celestial Bodies: The Cosmological Center

- **Sun** (*sol*): "The sun of righteousness shall rise with healing in its wings" (Malachi 4:2). The Church sings to Mary: *"From you has risen the Sun of Righteousness, Christ our God."* The sun is the visible image of Christ as the light and source of life for all creation.
- **Moon** (*luna*): *"His throne is like the sun before me, like the moon established forever, a faithful witness in the heavens"* (Ps. 89:37). In the hierarchy of lights, the moon, though secondary to the sun, reflects its light and governs the tides of earthly life.
- **Orient** (*Oriens*): In Zechariah 6:12, the Messiah is called simply "Oriens," the Rising One, the East from which light comes forth. This denotes Christ as the source of all illumination and renewal.

### The Planets and Their Influences

Zorzi argues that Christ merits the names of all the planets because he eminently possesses all their influences and effects:

- **Mercury** (*Mercurius*): Hermes, the divine interpreter. Christ exceeds all interpreters, for he declares: *"All things I have heard from my Father I have made known to you"* (John 15:15). The Incarnate Word is the supreme interpreter of divine mysteries.
- **Venus** (*Venus*): In her higher aspect, Venus represents love and benevolence. Christ exemplifies this "superior and benevolent Venus," showing forth the effect of love and charity above all things, giving his life for his beloved (John 15:12).
- **Mars** (*Mars*): The fierce judge and punisher of wrongdoers. The Father has given all judgment to the Son (John 5:22). Peter proclaims in Acts, *"He is the judge of the living and the dead"* (Acts 10:42). Christ is the true Mars, the avenging warrior against all sin and injustice.
- **Jupiter** (*Iuppiter*): Just, benevolent, and full of mercy. John writes, *"Grace and truth came through Jesus Christ"* (John 1:17). Jupiter is traditionally the planet of grace, law, and justice; Christ embodies these in perfection.
- **Saturn** (*Saturnus*): The true Sabbath and rest emanate from Christ. He promised, *"Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls"* (Matt. 11:28-29). Saturn represents the completion of time and the rest that comes with the fulfillment of all ages.

### Mountains and Geographical Features

Christ is the elevated mountain above the peaks of mountains (Micah 4:1; Ps. 68:16), the congealed (*coagulatus*) and fertile (*pinguis*) mountain in which God has chosen to dwell eternally, for "the Lord will dwell on this mountain forever" (Isa. 4:5). The mountain denotes transcendence, elevation above all earthly things, and the place of theophany where God is revealed.

### The Angel of the Covenant

In Malachi 3:1, after speaking of John the Baptist as the messenger (*angelus*) who prepares the way, the text continues: *"Behold, I send my messenger, and he will prepare the way before me. And the Lord whom you seek will suddenly come to his temple; and the messenger of the covenant in whom you delight, behold, he is coming."* Zorzi identifies this "angel of the covenant" (*angelus testamenti*) as Christ himself, though he is "better than the angels" (Heb. 1:4) and "Lord of the angels." As the incarnate Word, Christ is the supreme messenger (*angelus*) of God to humanity and the perfector of the covenant.

### The Supreme Being: *On* and *Ens Supersubstantiale*

Zorzi then reaches the apex of the hierarchy: Christ as *on* (being itself), in the language of Dionysius. Dionysius calls him the *on*, that is, *ens omnia entificans* (being that gives being to all), the supereminent cause, the foundatrix and principle of all things. <cite>p. 1585</cite> Plato in his *Epistles* uses the term *on* to denote the supreme intelligible reality. Hierotheus in the *Elementa theologiae* calls Christ the *supersubstantiale ens* (being-beyond-substance), the source that produces all essences and sustains all things. <cite>p. 1585</cite>

This is the culmination of Neoplatonic-Christian synthesis: the Greek philosophers intuited that there must be a principle beyond being, a ground of all being. In Christian revelation, this principle is identified with the Incarnate Word. Dionysius synthesizes the insight: "Totum esse (said Dionysius) in se pariter ambiens, et superapprehendens... rex saeculorum dicitur, et in quo, et circa quem totum esse, et ens, subsistensque versatur." <cite>p. 1585</cite> ("Encompassing and surpassing all being in itself... he is called king of the ages, in whom and around whom all being and entity exist and subsist.") Plato in his *Letter to Dionysius* touches upon this mystery without developing it fully. All these multifarious names converge on a single truth: Christ is the whole and each thing to which these names apply, because true names are not imposed by chance but according to the proper nature of the named, from the proto-plastes (first-creator) Adam who named all things in Genesis, as perceived by those who understand the property of the first language, now called Hebrew, in which were imposed those most accommodating names. The Greeks, "like apes," imitated this natural correctness of names to some extent, as Plato attempts to demonstrate in the *Cratylus*. <cite>p. 1585</cite>

---

## The Letter Beth and Kabbalistic Christology

Among the most evocative moments is Zorzi's discussion of the Hebrew letter *Beth* (ב). In Hebrew theology, the *Beth* is assigned as the seal (*sigillum*) of supreme Wisdom—that is, the sefirah *Chokmah*, identified with the Son. The letter *Beth* signifies *domus* (house), and Christ is the great house (*domus magna*) of God in which all the forms and images of things were stored before appearing in their proper subjects (*antequam in propriis appareret subiectis*). <cite>p. 1585</cite>

This house is built without saw, axe, hammer, or any iron implement (*absque serra, securi, malleo, et omni genere ferramenti*), because it is entirely filled with clemency, mercy, and benevolence (*referta clementia, pietate, beneficentia*), without any hardness of iron. In this house, sacrifices, oblations, and prayers are offered to the Father, fulfilling the promise: *"Whatever you ask the Father in my name, he will give you,"* for to ask in his name is to ask in his virtue and within the sphere of his operation. <cite>p. 1585</cite>

Christ is also the *tabernacle* (*tabernaculum*)—not the one made by human hands under Moses's direction, but the true tabernacle not of this creation. As Hebrews 8:2 states, Christ "is seated in the sanctuary, the true tabernacle set up by the Lord, not by any mortal." The Mosaic tabernacle was merely the shadow and type of this true, living tabernacle, which is Christ himself, the *domus* in which all divine and human worship is centered.

---

## The Theological Synthesis and Implications

The cumulative effect of Zorzi's encyclopedia of names is not to blur distinctions but to establish a profound principle of analogy and participation. Every creature, insofar as it is, participates in Christ. Every name correctly applied to a creature—stone, plant, animal, celestial body, or human faculty—can be applied to Christ in a "better sense" (*sensum meliorem*). This is the doctrine of *eminence* inherited from Neoplatonism: the cause contains its effects more perfectly than they possess themselves. Christ, as the *Verbum* (Word) through which all things were made and in whom all things consist (John 1:3; Col. 1:17), contains eminently all the perfections distributed among creatures.

Furthermore, this elaborate enumeration serves to establish the foundation for creaturely action. If Christ is truly all things and present in all things, then the faculty of operating (*facoltà di operare*) is grounded not in human initiative but in participation in Christ's operative presence. All action is, ultimately, Christ acting through the creature. This is the metaphysical ground for the ethical and spiritual claims that Zorzi will develop in the remainder of this tone: that obedience to divine law is obedience to Christ, that love of neighbor is love of Christ, that all virtue and sanctity flow from conformity to Christ's exemplary perfection.

In this way, Zorzi transforms what might appear as mere verbal proliferation—the endless enumeration of Christ's identities—into a rigorous metaphysical theology. The names are not synonymous; each captures a distinct mode of Christ's presence and operation. Yet they converge on a single truth: that the Incarnate Word is the principle and measure of all being, all knowledge, and all action. The universe is harmonious not because of abstract mathematical proportions or astronomical cycles, but because it is centered in Christ, the "harmony of all things" (*harmonia omnium rerum*).
